# Simon's Voice Guide (auto-generated from 32 posts)

---

## Hook Patterns

Simon uses 8 distinct hook types, roughly in order of frequency:

### 1. Personal vulnerability / difficulty statement
Opens by admitting struggle, uncertainty, or failure. No softening.
> "Q1 was the hardest quarter I've ever had since starting my business"
> "Every time a consulting client moved on, it felt like failure"
> "When I first started George St Growth, I didn't know what I wanted it to be"

### 2. Specific dollar / number + stakes
A precise number that signals credibility and creates instant scale.
> "I made a mistake that almost cost my company over $200,000"
> "In the past 5 years, I've spent over $1m+ on Out of Home campaigns"
> "I'll tell you a story I haven't shared before…how I made $25,000 on the internet as a high school student."

### 3. Pattern interrupt / unexpected outcome
Sets up an expectation, immediately breaks it.
> "I stopped posting on LinkedIn for 2 months and something interesting happened" → "I had my highest grossing month ever"
> "Going out on your own is so risky! / Is it?"

### 4. Milestone + personal reflection
Ties a date or anniversary to a broader reflection. No preamble.
> "2 years ago today, I said goodbye to my 6 figure salary"
> "Yesterday, I turned 30."
> "Today marks exactly 1 year since incorporating my business"
> "2024 was the year things finally started to click"

### 5. "One thing..." structure
Signals a single, focused insight upfront — not a listicle.
> "One of my biggest learnings from the past year? Get out of the house"
> "One area that's often overlooked when it comes to OOH"
> "One thing I wish I knew before going out on my own"

### 6. Declarative industry/trend statement
States a position directly. No hedging.
> "The trend around fractional roles is real and here to stay"

### 7. Story beat drop
Drops you into the middle of a scene or conversation.
> "I'm currently in Buenos Aires and went to a meetup last week where I met a fellow digital nomad who shared some great wisdom with me"

### 8. How-to / question direct
Lead with utility. Usually for tactical/OOH content.
> "How to turn Out of Home into a performance channel"
> "What exactly is a Fractional Head of Growth?"

**Hook length:** Typically 1 line before a hard line break. The hook is almost always a single declarative sentence. Rarely more than 15 words.

**Hook tone:** Direct, never teasing. It either states the outcome, stakes, or opens a specific question the post will answer.

---

## Structure

**Paragraph length:** Almost always 1–2 sentences, then a line break. Walls of text do not appear anywhere in the examples.

**Post formats observed:**

| Format | Posts | When used |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| Story arc (problem → action → outcome → lesson) | 1, 3, 7, 21, 30 | Personal experience / mistake posts |
| Milestone update + reflection | 4, 9, 12, 13, 14, 24, 32 | Anniversaries, year-in-reviews |
| Numbered list (3–5 items) | 9, 11, 16, 23, 28, 29 | Tactical or "things I learned" posts |
| Short observation → reframe | 6, 8, 19, 22 (second), 26 | Contrarian or insight posts |
| Pure update / sharing | 5, 10, 17, 25 | Network / community posts |

**Where payoff lands:** Almost always at the end — either a punchy one-liner, a lesson stated plainly, or a question to the reader. He builds to it; he doesn't open with the conclusion (except in the Direct Insight hook style).

**One idea per post:** True across almost every post. The tactical OOH posts are the exception (series), but even those stay tightly scoped per post.

---

## Voice Markers

### Vocabulary that repeats
- **"folks"** — used constantly (never "people" in isolation, always "folks" or "people + context")
- **"in a nutshell"** — appears in multiple posts as a summary signal
- **"double down"** — standard phrase for committing to something
- **"flywheel"** — his metaphor for compound business momentum
- **"trust the process"** — recurring theme, used earnestly
- **"level up"** — personal/professional growth context
- **"put in the reps"** — consistency / discipline metaphor
- **"luck surface area"** — original phrase, used once but very "him"
- **"pipeline"** — business development context
- **"ofc"** — casual shorthand, used in longer reflective posts

### Grammar / style quirks (intentional, preserve these)
- Missing apostrophes on contractions: "its" instead of "it's", "thats" instead of "that's", "whats" instead of "what's"
- "tldr" in lowercase as a genuine summary signal, not ironic
- Parenthetical asides in the middle of sentences: "(so far)", "(ish)", "(the worst!)", "(sometimes 😂)"
- Starting sentences with "And" and "But" — frequently and intentionally
- Single word or two-word sentences for emphasis: "But", "Is it?", "Thats weird.", "Huh."

### Formality level
Casual but precise. He uses marketing/business jargon (TAM, CAC, TOFU, OOH, FSA, FTE, B2C, SEM) without explaining it when writing for peers. He explains it when the post is educational.

### Hedging
Almost none. He states things directly. When he is uncertain, he says so explicitly ("my gut is telling me", "I know this is a stretch") rather than softening every claim.

### Humor
Dry and self-deprecating. Never at others' expense. Mostly via parentheticals: "(bookkeeping continues to be the absolute worst!)" — or a single 😂 emoji used sparingly. He doesn't try to be funny; it just comes out.

### Perspective
Heavy first person ("I", "my"). Rarely "we" unless referring to a specific team/client situation. Never third-person observations about "founders" or "marketers" in a detached way — he speaks from his own experience and invites the reader to relate.

### Personal references that ground the voice
- Cold plunges, swimming pool (health routines)
- Othership (wellness)
- Digital nomad life, travel (Colombia, Chile, Argentina, NYC)
- George St Growth (his company name — uses it by name)
- Bookkeeping as a recurring pain point / joke

---

## Endings

**5 closing patterns, roughly equal in frequency:**

1. **Punchy one-liner / lesson stated plainly**
   > "Trust yourself, trust the process and the rest will take care of itself."
   > "tldr, the fractional role is here to stay!"
   > "In the end, what matters is results."

2. **Motivational close** (brief, not generic)
   > "Keep going 💪"
   > "Much more to come"

3. **Question to the audience** (genuine, not engagement-bait)
   > "Just a me thing or do other folks feel the same?"
   > "Have you seen this work before? Any brands come to mind that have done this well?"

4. **Gratitude to clients** (appears in milestone posts, feels earned)
   > "A huge thank you to all my clients past and present for trusting me with your business."

5. **Soft CTA / open door**
   > "If you're thinking of taking the leap or already have, I'm always happy to chat to see how I can help!"
   > "Happy to intro or reach out directly!"

**What he never does to close:** A hard sell. A "drop a comment if you agree." A summary of what the post already said.

---

## What to Avoid

These patterns appear **zero times** in 32 posts:

- "Excited to share..." / "Excited to announce..."
- "Grateful for..." as an opener
- "In today's fast-paced world..."
- "Let that sink in."
- "Hot take:"
- "Unpopular opinion:"
- "This is your reminder to..."
- Excessive emoji (he uses 1–2 per post max, often zero)
- "Drop a 🔥 if you agree" or any engagement-bait
- Humble bragging without substance (he shares wins only with full context of the struggle)
- Hedging language: "might", "could potentially", "in some cases", "perhaps"
- Bullet points that all start with the same verb tense
- Inspirational quotes from other people
- Advice framed as universal truth without a personal story behind it — he almost always earns his insights through lived experience

---

## Tone Summary

Simon writes like he talks — casual but credible. He's a practitioner sharing real experiences, not a thought leader performing expertise. The vulnerability is genuine (he admits when things went badly, when he didn't know what he was doing). The confidence is earned (he backs claims with numbers and specifics). The warmth shows in how he talks about clients, the community, and people who helped him.

If in doubt: more specific, less polished.
